title: Pasteurellosis in rabbits: managing a chronic colony problem
subtitle: The bacterium behind snuffles, abscesses, and sudden deaths.
excerpt: Pasteurella multocida is present in most rabbit colonies and causes no problems until stress, immune suppression, or a dense population tips susceptible animals into disease. Snuffles (nasal discharge), jaw abscesses, and acute septicaemia are all expressions of the same organism. Management focuses on reducing stress and removing clinical cases rather than eliminating the bacterium entirely.
sourceNotes: Merck Veterinary Manual, pasteurellosis in rabbits (online edition). RWAF disease information, rwaf.org.uk. DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Rabbits (2019).

glossaryTerms.definition: A spectrum of diseases caused by Pasteurella multocida bacteria in rabbits, including snuffles (upper respiratory tract infection), conjunctivitis, ear infection, jaw abscess, and acute septicaemia. Most adult rabbits carry the organism subclinically; disease emerges when immune defences are reduced.

content.text: is one of the most common bacterial diseases in kept rabbit colonies. It cannot be eradicated once present. The goal is to keep individual animals healthy and disease at a low rate through good husbandry.

Snuffles: white, creamy, or greenish nasal discharge, sneezing, wet forepaws from wiping the nose. Upper respiratory tract infection is the most visible form. Jaw abscess: firm swelling under the chin or along the jaw that grows slowly over weeks and may rupture. The capsule of a Pasteurella abscess is thick and does not drain easily. Septicaemia: sudden death in a previously healthy animal, or rapid onset of lethargy, anorexia, and high temperature, often fatal within 24 to 48 hours.

Remove sick animals from the colony promptly. Persistent shedders infect others. Animals with snuffles can be treated with a long course of antibiotics from the vet. Treatment holds the infection down rather than clearing it. Animals that improve often relapse under stress.

Jaw and cheek abscesses need vet treatment. Surgical opening and flushing with antibiotic packing works better than simple lancing. Lancing allows rapid closure and re-infection. An abscess tied to a tooth root usually needs extraction to stop it coming back. Outcome depends on the location and how much bone is involved.

The main drivers of disease in a colony are crowding, poor ventilation (ammonia in the air irritates the nose lining), and stress from social conflict. Keep stocking low enough that all rabbits can reach food and water without fighting. Keep bedding dry and ammonia low. Cull animals that stay sick long-term rather than treating them indefinitely.
